title: Hailey Planked Headboard
summary: A framed, planked headboard with mitered casing trim and 2x4 legs sturdy enough to bolt a bed frame to. Full/queen width.
description:
A planked headboard in the framed-panel style: six 1x6 boards make up the field, wrapped in casing moulding for a picture-frame look, with 2x4 legs beefed up enough to bolt a standard metal bed frame to. At about 57-1/2" wide it suits a full or queen. The source didn't list tools, but the build is a straightforward glue-and-brad-nail job with mitered trim, so the tool list here is inferred from what the steps actually require.

Start by nailing the panel field to the back support boards — the nail holes on the back don't matter because the trim covers them, and if you have a pocket-hole jig you can also join the planks to each other down the center for a flatter panel. If your boards aren't perfectly co-planar, clamp them flat while the glue sets.

The legs do real work here. Beef them up with the 2x4 stock so there's meat to bolt a frame to, fastening from the back to hide screws. The moulding is the finish move: miter the corners at 45° and, for invisible joints, glue-and-clamp rather than nail — just let the glue fully set before handling. Finish with the top cap flush to the outside of the legs.

tools.note: The 45° moulding miters and the crosscuts.
Optional — joins the planks to each other for a flatter panel.
Clamp the panel flat and glue-clamp the miters for invisible joints.

materials.name: 2x4, 4 ft
materials.note: Leg beef-up for bolting a frame.

cutList.note: Cut to fit and miter corners at 45°.

steps.title: Build the planked panel
steps.body: Nail the panel boards to the back support boards; the nail holes on the back will be covered by trim. If you have a pocket-hole jig, also join the planks to each other down the center for a flatter panel. If the boards aren't lining up flush, clamp them flat while the glue sets.

steps.title: Add the bottom board
steps.body: Add the bottom board. Nail from the back for the biggest bite, or use screws and glue. Kreg-jig users can also attach with 3/4" pocket holes up from the bottom planked board.

steps.title: Beef up the legs
steps.body: Beef up the legs with the 2x4 stock so there's something solid to bolt a bed frame to if you're using one. Fasten with nails or screws from the back to keep the fasteners hidden.

steps.title: Miter and apply the moulding
steps.body: Cut the casing moulding to fit and miter the corners at 45° off square. Nail on with short finish nails and glue. For invisible joints, glue-and-clamp instead of nailing — but let the glue fully set before you handle the headboard.

steps.title: Add the top and finish
steps.body: Add the top rail and trim, keeping the outsides flush with the legs. Sand and finish — paint or stain both suit the framed-panel look.

images.alt: Framed planked headboard with mitered casing trim for a full or queen bed
